groundsForSuspicion: Large inbound credit followed by a similar-value outbound debit within a short time frame (11 hours).

No transactions consistent with a stated economic purpose; the account functions solely as a conduit.

strDraft.activitySummary: Account 1813079910 was opened on an unknown date and received approximately £97,000 shortly thereafter. Over nine days starting 2022-10-16, a series of 11 outbound transfers totalling £97,929.07 were made to numerous unrelated third-party accounts, leaving a residual balance of £5,000. There is no evidence of any legitimate economic purpose for these transactions, and the account appears to have served solely as a temporary conduit for funds.
groundsForSuspicion: Account was newly opened (likely within 8 days of first transaction) and exhibited rapid pass-through activity.
Incoming funds of ~£97,000 were almost entirely depleted within 9 days via multiple transfers to unrelated parties, leaving only £5,000.
No spending or transactions consistent with a stated business or personal purpose; funds were not retained or used for legitimate expenses.
The pattern is indicative of a pass-through scheme where the account is used to quickly move illicit funds and obscure their origin.
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Pass-through / Rapid Movement pattern.

trigger: 12 transactions (10 in / 2 out) across 11 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: The account received 10 inbound transfers from 10 different counterparties over 13 days, aggregating to a high balance, followed by a large outbound transfer. This pattern matches Fan-in/Fan-out consolidation, with no apparent relationship among the senders, and the behavior is inconsistent with typical personal account usage.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

indicators: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one or few large outbound transfers
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile
fired: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one or few large outbound transfers
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder

verifier.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

challenge.counterHypothesis: Legitimate merchant or business account receiving payments for goods/services.
challenge.distinguishingTestAssessment: The evidence does not satisfy the mule consolidation pattern because: (1) the outbound transactions are not rapid full forwarding—only two outbounds, one before any inbound and one partial; (2) the account retains a significant working balance (retained >86k after outbound); (3) the inbound amounts are large and varied, not typical of many small customer payments; (4) the counterparty Acct 3456018904 appears both as sender and recipient, suggesting a possible refund or legitimate business relationship; (5) we lack information on whether senders are personal or business, and the account type is unknown.

rebuttal.argument: The evidence shows a retained working balance over 86k, an outbound before any inbound, and a counterparty appearing as both sender and recipient, which aligns better with a legitimate business than rapid mule consolidation.

reverdict.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

challenge.distinguishingTestAssessment: sendersAreUnrelatedPersonal: Unknown; counterparties are diverse but no evidence they are personal accounts.; rapidFullForwarding: Not present; no outbound transactions occur.; personalAccountType: Account type is unknown (SAML-D), not specified as personal.; retainedWorkingBalance: True; balance increases and is not forwarded.; consistentAmounts: Partially; amounts vary but within a range typical for small business payments.

The verifier's counter-hypothesis is more consistent.

reverdict.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

challenge.distinguishingTestAssessment: consistent amounts: Satisfied partially: most inbound amounts are around 4700 GBP, showing consistency.; business account type: Not determined: account type is unknown, but could be business.; retained/working balance: Satisfied: running balance shows retained funds after outbound transfers, not fully forwarded.; unrelated personal senders: Not satisfied: senders may be customers (related by business).; rapid full forwarding: Not satisfied: outbound are partial and not immediate; there are delays and balance accumulation.; personal account type: Not satisfied: account type unknown; no evidence it is personal.

rebuttal.argument: The evidence shows consistent inbound amounts, retained working balance, and no rapid full forwarding or clear personal account usage, fitting a legitimate merchant better than mule consolidation.

reverdict.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

challenge.counterHypothesis: Legitimate merchant or crowdfunding account receiving many small customer payments.

trigger: 12 transactions (11 in / 1 out) across 9 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: Account opened 2022-10-07 with no KYC information; immediately after, it receives 11 inbound transfers totaling over 82,000 GBP within 6 hours from 9 different counterparties. This throughput far exceeds expected activity for a newly opened personal account, indicating a potential KYC profile mismatch.

matchedTypology.name: KYC Profile Mismatch
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

indicators: Throughput far exceeds declared income or expected activity
Transaction types inconsistent with account type (e.g. heavy international wires on a basic personal account)
Counterparties or geographies inconsistent with the customer profile
Stated occupation does not explain the flows
fired: Throughput far exceeds declared income or expected activity

verifier.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

challenge.counterHypothesis: The customer opened the account specifically to receive multiple payments from various clients (e.g., as a freelancer or small business) and can provide invoices or contracts for each transaction. The initial outbound may be a payment to a supplier or a test transaction. All transactions are documentable and legitimate.
challenge.distinguishingTestAssessment: The distinguishing test states that a mismatch is flagged when the activity has NO consistent explanation against the known profile and no updated CDD on file. In this case, the profile is unknown, but the activity can be consistently explained as legitimate business receipts. The timing and pattern, while unusual, are not inherently inconsistent with a genuine business consolidation event. Furthermore, the customer could provide documentation to support each transaction. Therefore, the evidence does NOT satisfy the 'no consistent explanation' part of the test, making the escalation premature.

rebuttal.argument: The account has no established profile, so the transactions cannot be deemed inconsistent with any known profile. The pattern is readily explainable as a business receiving client payments, and documentation could support this, satisfying the distinguishing test for a genuine profile.

reverdict.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: A newly opened account received 12 inbound credits from 10 distinct counterparty accounts within 3.5 hours, accumulating over £140,000, with no apparent relationship to the account holder. This rapid aggregation of funds from multiple unrelated individuals strongly indicates a consolidation phase, likely preceding onward transfers consistent with money mule activity.
groundsForSuspicion: 12 inbound credits from 10 distinct counterparties in under 4 hours.
Counterparties have no evident relationship to the account holder.
Aggregation of over £140,000 without any outgoing transfers to date.
Mixed currency transaction (Albanian lek) suggests cross-border layering.
Pattern matches known fan-in consolidation typology used by money mules.
Account holder profile (SAML-D) inconsistent with high-volume personal transactions.
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation) pattern.

trigger: 5 transactions (0 in / 5 out) across 1 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: No candidate typology indicators present in the evidence. The account shows only outbound transfers to a single counterparty over several months after an initial credit, which does not match the pass-through, fan-in, structuring, dormant-then-active, or KYC mismatch patterns.

verifier.note: No candidate typology matched the evidence; no laundering pattern to escalate.

trigger: 4 transactions (2 in / 2 out) across 1 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: No typology card matches the evidence. The transactions show two cycles of inbound PKR followed by outbound GBP with delays of 51 and 47 days, which does not meet the 'short window' indicator of PT-01. The amounts, while numerically close, are in different currencies with vastly different values, so the 'similar amount' indicator also fails. Other typologies are not supported: no multiple inbound from unrelated parties (FI-01), no cash deposits or structuring (ST-01), no dormancy (DA-01), and insufficient KYC data to assess profile mismatch (KYC-01).

verifier.note: No candidate typology matched the evidence; no laundering pattern to escalate.

trigger: 11 transactions (0 in / 11 out) across 1 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: No candidate typology matches the evidence. Only outbound transactions to a single counterparty are observed, with no inbound credits, structuring, dormant activity, or KYC profile information.

verifier.note: No candidate typology matched the evidence; no laundering pattern to escalate.

trigger: 12 transactions (12 in / 0 out) across 1 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: The alert shows a newly opened account receiving 12 inbound transfers from a single counterparty within a day, with no outbound transactions. None of the candidate typologies fit: Pass-through requires outbound; Fan-in requires multiple unrelated senders; Structuring specifies cash deposits; Dormant requires prior inactivity; KYC requires profile information not provided.

verifier.note: No candidate typology matched the evidence; no laundering pattern to escalate.

trigger: 6 transactions (6 in / 0 out) across 1 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: Alert shows only inbound credits from a single counterparty over many months with no outbound transactions, no rapid movement, no structuring, no fan-in pattern, no dormant-then-active activity, and no KYC profile data to suggest mismatch. None of the candidate typologies are present.

verifier.note: No candidate typology matched the evidence; no laundering pattern to escalate.

trigger: 12 transactions (0 in / 12 out) across 1 counterparties on this account flagged by transaction monitoring

triage.explanation: Account 638906690 was opened on 2023-07-05 and immediately received a large deposit (approx. 61,500 UK pounds), followed by 12 rapid outbound transfers to a single counterparty within the same day, each around 4,700 pounds, draining the balance to 5,000. This matches PT-01 pass-through/rapid movement: inbound credit, similar outbound transfers in a short window, balance spike then drain, newly opened account, and minimal funds retained.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

indicators: Inbound credit followed by outbound debit of a similar amount within a short window (minutes to a few days)
runningBalance spikes on the inbound then drains back to ~0
Little to no funds retained; no spending consistent with the stated purpose of the account
Often a newly opened or recently dormant account
fired: Inbound credit followed by outbound debit of a similar amount within a short window (minutes to a few days)
runningBalance spikes on the inbound then drains back to ~0
Little to no funds retained; no spending consistent with the stated purpose of the account
Often a newly opened or recently dormant account

verifier.note: Pattern shows multiple outbound transfers to single counterparty on same day with irregular timing, no commercial rationale, and account opened same day—consistent with pass-through laundering rather than regular benign sweeps.

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: On 5 July 2023, a newly opened account (638906690) received an inbound credit of approximately £61,500. Within hours, the account executed 12 rapid outbound transfers to a single external account (915444634), each around £4,700, leaving a minimal balance of £5,000. No other activity occurred, and the funds were moved without any apparent legitimate economic purpose.
groundsForSuspicion: Account opened same day as the transactions, with no prior history.
Inbound credit of ~£61,500 followed by 12 outbound debits totaling ~£56,500 within hours, all to the same counterparty.
Running balance spiked then rapidly drained to near zero (£5,000), indicating a pass-through/layering pattern.
No funds were retained, and no spending consistent with a stated purpose; the activity lacks economic rationale.
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Pass-through / Rapid Movement pattern.

trigger: Several inbound transfers from different individuals consolidated, then a payment forwarded onward; flagged by the fan-in velocity rule

triage.explanation: The account received multiple inbound credits from unrelated individuals within a short timeframe, consolidating funds before a single large outbound transfer. This pattern matches the fan-in typology, indicating potential money mule or layering activity. Escalation recommended for further investigation.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

indicators: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one or few large outbound transfers
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile
fired: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one or few large outbound transfers
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile

verifier.note: Account shows retention of funds, partial forwarding to a business counterparty, and consistent inbound amounts, consistent with a legitimate merchant receiving customer payments rather than mule consolidation.

challenge.counterHypothesis: A legitimate merchant consolidating customer payments: funds are partly retained and forwarded to an established business counterparty, not swept out in full to an unrelated beneficiary.
challenge.distinguishingTestAssessment: The fan-in test wants rapid, near-complete pass-through from unrelated senders to an unrelated beneficiary with the balance drained to ~0. Here the balance is retained, forwarding is partial and to a known business counterparty, and inbound amounts are consistent — so the distinguishing test is not cleanly satisfied.

rebuttal.argument: The inbound credits come from multiple UNRELATED individuals rather than repeat, identifiable customers, and they consolidate into a single large outbound within a short window — the fan-in layering signature. Partial retention is consistent with staged forwarding, not a merchant margin.

reverdict.note: The unrelated-sender consolidation is suggestive, but the retained balance and forwarding to an established business counterparty leave the fan-in distinguishing test unsatisfied. The flag holds — refer to a human analyst before acting.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: The account of Aisyah binti Kamal received five inbound transfers totaling MYR 4,300 from individuals with distinct surnames and no apparent relationship, over an 11-day period. These funds were aggregated to a peak balance of MYR 7,450, then a single MYR 2,000 outbound transfer was made to 'Ilmu Educational Supplies Sdn Bhd', reducing the balance to MYR 5,450. The rapid, structured consolidation and onward transfer pattern is inconsistent with the expected activity of a personal account holder.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple inbound credits from unrelated individuals in a short timeframe
Funds aggregated and quickly sent onward in a large outbound transfer
Senders have no apparent connection to account holder
Transaction pattern inconsistent with personal account profile
Amounts are uniform (MYR 850-900), suggesting structured layering
strDraft.recommendedAction: Hold for analyst confirmation before filing — the verifier flagged this Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation) call; escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) only once the pattern is substantiated.

trigger: International wire volume grossly inconsistent with the customer's declared student profile and zero declared income; no CDD update on file

account.holderName: Muhammad Faiz bin Razak (declared: full-time student, income RM0)

triage.explanation: Account shows a classic pass-through pattern: a large inbound wire (RM95k) is followed within hours by an outbound wire of similar amount (RM88k), leaving minimal balance. The account is new (4 months) and the activity is inconsistent with a student profile, suggesting rapid movement of funds with no economic purpose.

subject.holderName: Muhammad Faiz bin Razak (declared: full-time student, income RM0)

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: On 11 June 2026, an inbound wire of MYR 95,000 was received from Gulf Star Remittance (Dubai), followed within 2.5 hours by an outbound wire of MYR 88,000 to Apex OTC Trading (Cayman). The account balance then returned near zero after a cash withdrawal of MYR 6,800 on 12 June 2026, leaving only MYR 500. The rapid movement of funds through a new student account with no declared income lacks any apparent economic purpose.
groundsForSuspicion: Inbound credit of MYR 95,000 followed by outbound debit of MYR 88,000 within 2.5 hours (rapid pass-through).
Account balance returned to near-zero (MYR 500) after subsequent cash withdrawal.
Activity inconsistent with account holder's profile: full-time student with declared income of MYR 0.
No funds retained; no spending consistent with the stated account purpose.
Newly opened account (4 months) with high-value wire activity.
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Pass-through / Rapid Movement pattern.

trigger: Numerous inbound transfers from unrelated individuals rapidly consolidated and forwarded in full to a single offshore party

triage.explanation: The account received multiple rapid inbound transfers from different individuals (HT-3001 to HT-3005) within 3 hours, then immediately forwarded nearly the full aggregated amount offshore (HT-3006). This matches the fan-in/consolidation pattern typical of money mule activity.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)

indicators: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one or few large outbound transfers
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile
fired: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one or few large outbound transfers
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile

verifier.note: Evidence matches mule consolidation: personal account, unrelated individual senders, rapid full forwarding to offshore party, no business context.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: On 14 June 2026, Kevin Ng Jun Hao's account received five rapid credits totaling MYR 24,400 from five unrelated individuals (Goh Pei Shan, Ahmad Zaki bin Othman, Chong Li Wei, Siti Noraini binti Aziz, Daniel Tan Wei Sheng) between 09:05 and 11:58. Within two hours, MYR 24,000 (98% of the accumulated funds) was wired to an offshore entity, Hai Feng Trading. The pattern is inconsistent with the holder's expected low-volume personal account activity and exhibits classic fan-in consolidation indicative of money mule behavior.
groundsForSuspicion: Five inbound credits from different, unrelated individual senders in under 3 hours
All inbound amounts are round figures just below MYR 5,000, potentially avoiding scrutiny or reporting thresholds
Funds were immediately aggregated and forwarded in a single large outbound transfer to an offshore company
Senders have no apparent personal or business relationship to account holder Kevin Ng Jun Hao
Transaction pattern and velocity are wholly inconsistent with a personal account's expected usage
Outbound transfer left a negligible remaining balance (MYR 700), consistent with a conduit account
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation) pattern.
